attributes.stem:
A 56-year-old man is brought to the Emergency Department after a witnessed seizure. He was talking with friends in his workshop when he suddenly fell to the floor. He had limb-jerking movements, loss of consciousness, and both urinary and faecal incontinence. He was confused initially, but by the time he arrived in the Emergency Department he was back to his normal self. He has no aura and no complaints at this time. His medical history is unremarkable apart from a head injury involving an improvised explosive device which he sustained twelve years ago whilst serving in the Defence Force. He is not on any medications.

On examination his blood pressure is 126/82 mmHg, pulse 94/min, temperature 37.3˚C and Sa02 98% on room air. His pupils are normal in size and reactive to light. The rest of the physical examination is normal and there is no neurologic deficit. An ECG, his basic laboratory investigations, including a blood alcohol estimation and cervical spine X-rays are all normal. A urine toxicology screen is ordered.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has had a witnessed tonic-clonic seizure. Initial stabilisation in the Emergency Department has been achieved and he is not at risk for status epilepticus. Now that his condition appears to be stabilised, a cause for the seizure must be sought. Urine drug screen and blood alcohol levels are essential. The initial investigations have not revealed an obvious cause. Whilst it is a remote chance, he is at risk for traumatic brain injury from the explosion he was involved in. Thus some sort of brain imaging is indicated. A CT scan head is best because it is fast and can identify bleeds.

Lumbar puncture (LP) is indicated when there is a seizure and a fever, and suspicion of an infection. In such circumstances a CT scan of the head is still required prior to LP to exclude raised intracranial pressure. Given his normal vital signs, the absence of any meningeal irritation and absence of focal neurologic deficits, the chance of this being infectious is quite low.

24-hours EEG telemetry is used to determine the nature of seizures especially when normal EEG results are inconclusive because the patient is presenting to the hospital after their seizure is over. The patient is admitted to the hospital and a continuous EEG recording machine is attached along with a camera that is recording the patient. Then the doctor can correlate the findings on the EEG with the physical manifestations that it is causing. This can be a test used for diagnosis, but it is usually used when the patient presents with multiple seizures and the initial tests are inconclusive to find out the cause of the seizures.

SPECT (Single photon emission computed tomography) is an advanced diagnostic tool and it is usually used for presurgical evaluation of epileptic onset zone in patients with intractable partial epilepsy. Functional imaging with SPECT reflects seizure related changes of cerebral perfusion, glucose-metabolism and neuroreceptor status.

MRI has the best soft tissue visibility and it can be used when an intracranial lesion such as a tumour is suspected. However a CT scan is a more convenient and easily accessible imaging tool and most of the lesions responsible a seizure can be visualised on a plain or contrast-enhanced CT scan. A contrast MRI is indicated if the CT head scan is unremarkable. Considering the injury previously sustained, the possibility of a retained metallic foreign body should be considered as this may mitigate against an MRI.
references: Bergey GK. Management of a First Seizure. Continuum (Minneap Minn). 2016 Feb;22(1 Epilepsy):38-50. doi: 10.1212/CON.0000000000000271. PMID: 26844729.
